# **The Helix Framework**

## Balancing Purpose and Profit in Healthcare Research

[Shahin Mohammadi](mailto:mohammadi@cytognosis.org)

## 

## Abstract

For centuries, humanity has faced a false choice: whether to pursue profit or purpose; build sustainable businesses or build equitable societies; achieve financial returns or achieve social impact. The Helix Framework reveals this dichotomy as an illusion. Just as ancient Chinese philosophy recognized that yin and yang are not opposing forces but complementary aspects of a unified whole—each containing the seed of the other, each defining the other through their dance—profit and purpose are two strands of the same organizational DNA. Like light's wave-particle duality that revolutionized physics by proving the same phenomenon could be simultaneously two seemingly incompatible things, modern organizations can be both commercially viable and charitably transformative without contradiction. The Helix Framework introduces recipes to structure the future generation of hybrid organizations, where doing good and doing well are not parallel paths but intertwined strands of the same revolutionary DNA. Through a comprehensive analysis of existing innovation models, we learn what has worked, identify critical gaps, and propose alternative paths forward for mission-first, high-impact organizations. The Cytognosis Foundation serves as an instance of the Hellix Model, demonstrating practical application of these principles in creating mission-locked organizations that attract substantial capital while maintaining an unwavering commitment to global health equity.

![][image1]

## 1\. Comparative Analysis of Existing Innovation Models

**Timeline:**

* Focused Research Organizations (FROs) [(Marblestone 2022\)](https://app.readcube.com/library/8d0606e3-4d54-4bc6-ae5b-bb6a8e27f324/all?uuid=6534857737678823&item_ids=8d0606e3-4d54-4bc6-ae5b-bb6a8e27f324:313172cd-8869-4ba4-bde5-9d002236223d)  
* 

### 1.1 ARPA-Like Models: Empowering Visionary Program Managers

The Advanced Research Projects Agency (ARPA) model, pioneered by DARPA in 1958, represents one of the most successful frameworks for driving breakthrough innovation. DARPA's approach—"shifting the impossible to the inevitable"—has yielded transformative technologies, ranging from the internet to GPS to mRNA vaccines. The model's core innovation lies not in the funding amount, but in its organizational structure and operational philosophy.

#### Core Principles of ARPA Models

DARPA operates through empowered program managers who possess extraordinary autonomy to define and pursue ambitious technical challenges. These managers, typically recruited from academia or industry for 3- to 5-year tours, control program budgets ranging from $10 million to $100 million without requiring multiple layers of bureaucratic approval. The flat organizational hierarchy, comprising just three levels —from director to program manager—enables rapid decision-making and course correction.

The agency embraces failure as a necessary component of breakthrough innovation. Programs are designed to test limits rather than guarantee success, recognizing that even failed programs generate valuable knowledge and insights. This risk tolerance, combined with active program management in which managers work closely with performers rather than simply monitoring progress, creates an environment where revolutionary advances are possible.

#### Private ARPA Initiatives

The success of government ARPA programs has inspired private sector adaptations. Private ARPA (PARPA) initiatives seek to replicate DARPA's innovation model outside government constraints. These organizations maintain the core ARPA principles—empowered program managers, ambitious goals, and risk tolerance—while adapting to private-sector contexts.

Key distinctions of private ARPA models (PARPAs) include flexible funding sources beyond congressional appropriations, the ability to retain equity or IP rights in developments, faster iteration cycles without procurement regulations, and direct pathways to commercialization. However, PARPAs face challenges in matching the scale of government, maintaining a long-term vision without quarterly pressures, and building legitimacy without government authority.

#### Variations and Evolution

ARPA-E (2009) adapted the model to address energy challenges, focusing on transitioning technology to the market. ARPA-H (2022) focuses on health breakthroughs, allocating an initial $2.5 billion across distributed hubs. IARPA pursues high-risk intelligence research. Each variant maintains core ARPA principles while adapting to domain-specific needs.

The model's strength lies in its program manager-centric approach, in which individual vision and expertise drive portfolio development. However, this dependence on exceptional individuals creates scalability challenges and institutional knowledge loss when managers rotate out of their positions.

### 1.2 Focused Research Organizations: Targeted Bottleneck Resolution

Focused Research Organizations (FROs) [1](https://app.readcube.com/library/8d0606e3-4d54-4bc6-ae5b-bb6a8e27f324/all?uuid=1930525883419203&item_ids=8d0606e3-4d54-4bc6-ae5b-bb6a8e27f324:313172cd-8869-4ba4-bde5-9d002236223d) have recently emerged to address a critical gap: research projects that are too large for academic labs but unsuitable for commercial ventures. FROs are time-limited nonprofits designed to solve specific technical bottlenecks through coordinated engineering efforts.

#### 

#### The FRO Model Architecture

FROs operate on three foundational principles. First, they pursue prespecified quantifiable milestones rather than open-ended research, ensuring clear success metrics. Second, they maintain finite 5-7 year durations to preserve focus and urgency. Third, they employ teams of 10-30+ full-time scientists and engineers with competitive compensation, recognizing that breakthrough innovation requires dedicated effort beyond that of graduate students.

![][image2]

**Focused Research Organizations (FROs) bridge the gap between government funders and academic research for the public good and industry’s focused development practices, delivering humanity's mission-critical needs with a sense of urgency.** 

**(This figure is adopted from the [Convergent Research website](https://www.convergentresearch.org/about-fros) and needs to be recreated.)**

The model fills a crucial gap in the innovation ecosystem. Academic labs excel at discovery but often lack the resources necessary for sustained engineering efforts. Companies focus on profitable applications rather than foundational tools. Government labs operate within bureaucratic constraints. FROs provide an alternative: mission-driven organizations with startup-like execution pursuing public goods.

Convergent Research, founded by Adam Marblestone and others, serves as the primary incubator for FROs. They provide operational infrastructure, fundraising support, and shared learning across portfolio organizations. Since 2021, Convergent Research has launched multiple FROs, each with $20-50 million in funding from philanthropic sources, including Schmidt Futures, Open Philanthropy, and private donors.

#### Distinguishing Features from ARPA Models

While both FROs and ARPA programs pursue ambitious technical goals, fundamental differences distinguish these approaches. FROs operate as independent organizations rather than programs within agencies, enabling sustained focus on single problems rather than portfolio management. They emphasize organizational building over project funding, creating dedicated teams rather than coordinating external performers.

FROs prioritize open science and tool development over classified or proprietary outcomes. Their nonprofit structure ensures public benefit orientation, while finite timelines prevent mission drift. However, FROs face challenges in talent retention without equity compensation, difficulty transitioning from research to deployment phases, and sustainability questions as the conclusion approaches.

The FRO model excels at concentrated efforts on well-defined problems but struggles with scope expansion and long-term institutional building. The time-bound nature that ensures focus also creates instability as projects near completion.

### 1.3 Wellcome Leap: Global Health Moonshots

Wellcome Leap, launched in 2020 with initial funding from Wellcome Trust's £36.7 billion endowment, represents an ambitious adaptation of the DARPA model for global health challenges. Led by former DARPA Director Regina Dugan and former White House Science Advisor Kareen Gabriel, Wellcome Leap pursues "moonshot" programs to advance breakthrough health outcomes with 5- to 10-year horizons.

#### Innovation Model and Pasteur's Quadrant

Wellcome Leap operates in "Pasteur's Quadrant"—pursuing research that advances fundamental understanding while solving practical problems. This positioning distinguishes it from pure basic research (Bohr's Quadrant) or pure applied research (Edison's Quadrant). Programs must demonstrate both scientific breakthrough potential and clear paths to global health impact.

The organization's innovation model centers on program-driven research, where bold hypotheses define success, rather than incremental progress. Programs begin with aspirational goals—like creating bioengineered organs or developing global vaccine manufacturing capacity—then work backward to identify critical challenges and technical milestones. This "moonshot first" approach drives ambition beyond typical grant-making.

![][image3]

**Illustration of Pasteur’s Quadrant [2](https://app.readcube.com/library/8d0606e3-4d54-4bc6-ae5b-bb6a8e27f324/all?uuid=7258818809547268&item_ids=8d0606e3-4d54-4bc6-ae5b-bb6a8e27f324:ab9064c5-4bfd-40a4-92bc-3330d93fb2ab), the driving force of innovation in APRA-like models and the core of the Wellcome Leap’s Innovation Model.** 

Active program management ensures rapid iteration and course correction. Program directors—recruited globally for three-year terms—maintain close relationships with performers, adjusting milestones and resources in response to emerging results. This hands-on approach contrasts with traditional "fire and forget" grant-making, where funders provide money but limited ongoing engagement.

#### Implementation Through HBNet and Funding Agreements

Wellcome Leap's Health Breakthrough Network (HBNet) creates a global ecosystem of performers, partners, and capabilities. Rather than building centralized facilities, HBNet leverages distributed resources across academic, commercial, and government organizations worldwide. This network approach enables rapid scaling and geographic diversity while minimizing infrastructure overhead.

The Master Academic Research Funding Agreement (MARFA) and the Commercial Research Funding Agreement (CORFA) provide standardized frameworks that balance open science with commercialization potential. Key provisions include:

- Background and foreground IP distinctions protecting prior innovations while ensuring program advances  
- Publication rights with embargo periods for patent filing  
- Step-in rights ensuring Wellcome Leap can advance technologies if performers don't pursue commercialization  
- Milestone-based extensions beyond initial terms based on progress  
- Clear commercialization pathways with predetermined terms

These agreements address a critical challenge: ensuring that breakthrough innovations reach global deployment, regardless of the performer's decisions. The step-in rights and commercialization provisions prevent technologies from languishing unused while respecting performers' contributions and incentives.

Programs launched to date demonstrate an ambitious scope, with investments of $50 million each in bioengineered organ development, distributed vaccine biofoundries, infant brain development tools, and women's health initiatives, totaling $150 million. Each program brings together multiple performers from different countries, creating collaborative rather than competitive dynamics.

### 1.4 Alternative Innovation Models

Beyond ARPA-like programs, FROs, and Wellcome Leap, several alternative models address innovation challenges through different approaches.

#### Science Philanthropy Initiatives

Organizations such as Schmidt Futures, Open Philanthropy, and Arnold Ventures deploy billions of dollars in strategic scientific funding. These philanthropies operate between traditional foundations and venture capital, providing patient capital for high-risk research while expecting measurable impact. Their flexibility enables support for unconventional approaches, institutional experiments, and long-term capacity building.

#### Public-Private Partnerships

Initiatives such as the Cancer Moonshot, the Partnership for AI, and the Coalition for Epidemic Preparedness Innovations (CEPI) leverage government, industry, and philanthropic resources. These partnerships leverage diverse capabilities while sharing risks and rewards; however, the complexity of coordination and conflicting incentives often hinders progress.

#### Innovation Institutes

The MIT Media Lab, the Santa Fe Institute, and the Allen Institute for AI pursue interdisciplinary research through permanent institutional structures. These organizations provide stable environments for long-term investigation while maintaining flexibility to pursue emerging opportunities. Their challenge lies in striking a balance between institutional sustainability and the freedom of research.

#### Venture Philanthropy

Organizations like New Science and Actuate blur boundaries between investment and philanthropy. They provide funding at below-market returns, enabling support for ventures with significant public benefit but limited commercial potential. This model attracts mission-aligned capital while maintaining some sustainability mechanisms.

Each alternative model offers unique advantages but also faces limitations. Science philanthropy relies on the continued commitment of wealthy individuals. Public-private partnerships struggle with bureaucracy. Innovation institutes risk institutional sclerosis. Venture philanthropy faces scale constraints. Understanding these trade-offs informs the development of new frameworks combining strengths while addressing weaknesses.

## 2\. Case Studies of Innovation Organizations

### 2.1 Focused Research Organizations in Practice

#### E11 Bio: Mapping Neural Circuits

E11 Bio exemplifies the FRO model's potential for tackling foundational biological challenges. Founded in 2023 with $32 million in funding from Schmidt Futures and other investors, E11 Bio develops tools to understand brain wiring, accelerating neuroscience and enhancing human health.

The organization focuses on creating comprehensive maps of neural circuits—the connections between neurons that underlie all brain function. Current techniques can map either large areas at low resolution or tiny regions at high resolution, but not both. E11 Bio develops technologies bridging this gap, enabling researchers to understand how neural circuits produce behavior and how their disruption causes disease.

E11 Bio's approach demonstrates FRO principles in action. The team comprises over 25 full-time engineers and scientists working toward specific five-year milestones: mapping the entire mouse brain circuit at synaptic resolution, developing automated analysis pipelines for circuit data, and creating open-source tools for the neuroscience community. By focusing exclusively on this bottleneck, E11 Bio can pursue approaches too resource-intensive for academic labs but too foundational for companies.

#### Forest Neurotech: Building Brain-Computer Interfaces

Forest Neurotech pursues the development of implanted whole-brain interfaces through coordinated engineering efforts impossible in traditional settings. With $20 million in philanthropic funding, Forest brings together experts in materials science, electrical engineering, neuroscience, and surgical robotics. The organization's Microdot Array technology represents a breakthrough in neural interface design. Traditional brain-computer interfaces either cover small areas with high resolution or large areas with low resolution. Forest's approach uses thousands of tiny sensors distributed across the brain surface, providing unprecedented coverage while maintaining signal quality.

Forest demonstrates how FROs enable rapid iteration on hardware challenges. The team has progressed from concept to functional prototypes in under two years, a timeline impossible in academic settings with teaching obligations and grant cycles. Their full-time engineering focus and integrated team structure accelerate development beyond what distributed collaborations achieve.

#### IMPRINT: Decoding Immunity

IMPRINT (Immune Memory and Profiling Research Initiative) tackles one of biomedicine's grand challenges: understanding the language of the immune system. With $30 million in funding, IMPRINT develops technologies to read immune memory—the record of every infection, vaccination, and immune response encoded in our cells.

The organization pursues three integrated objectives: developing high-throughput methods for analyzing immune repertoires, creating computational models predicting immune responses, and building open databases of immune signatures. This coordinated approach requires expertise spanning immunology, genomics, machine learning, and clinical medicine—a combination difficult to sustain in traditional research structures.

IMPRINT's progress demonstrates FRO advantages. In 18 months, they've developed novel sequencing techniques that increased throughput 100-fold, created machine learning models that predict vaccine responses with 85% accuracy, and established partnerships with hospitals across three continents for sample collection. This rapid advancement stems from dedicated teams pursuing aligned objectives without competing obligations.

### 2.2 Hybrid Organizations: Navigating Dual Missions

#### Chan Zuckerberg Initiative: Flexible Philanthropy

CZI's structure as an LLC rather than a traditional foundation enables unprecedented operational flexibility. Since 2015, CZI has deployed $7.22 billion across science, education, and community initiatives. The organization can make grants, invest in companies, fund lobbying efforts, and operate programs directly—capabilities impossible for traditional foundations.

In science, CZI's investments demonstrate the unique potential of the hybrid model. The Chan Zuckerberg Biohub Network ($1.5 billion committed) operates as nonprofit research institutes with investigator freedom and open science commitments. CZI Science initiatives, such as the Human Cell Atlas and infectious disease programs, combine grant-making with technology development and community building.

However, CZI's structure also reveals challenges to the hybrid model. The LLC format provides no tax benefits for donors and limited external accountability. Dependence on founders' wealth raises questions about sustainability. The lack of clear succession planning creates uncertainty about long-term commitment to the mission. These limitations highlight the importance of structural safeguards in hybrid models.

#### OpenAI: From Nonprofit to Capped-Profit

OpenAI's evolution from a pure nonprofit (2015) to a capped-profit subsidiary (2019) to a planned Public Benefit Corporation (2025) illustrates both the opportunities and challenges in hybrid structures. The organization successfully raised over $13 billion while maintaining nominal nonprofit control, proving that investors accept limited returns in exchange for participation in breakthrough technology.

The capped-profit model pioneered core innovative concepts. Return caps decreasing with investment timing (100x for early investors, lower for later stages) align risk and reward. Nonprofit board control preserves mission primacy. AGI exclusion from commercial licenses protects humanity's interests.

However, OpenAI's struggles reveal structural weaknesses. The absence of a Public Benefit Corporation structure from inception left mission enforcement dependent on board governance rather than legal requirements. Current legal challenges to restructuring demonstrate the difficulty of retroactive changes. The model's complexity creates confusion among stakeholders and potential conflicts.

#### Pasteur Labs and Institute for Simulation Intelligence

Pasteur Labs operates as a venture-backed Public Benefit Corporation alongside the Institute for Simulation Intelligence, a 501(c)(3) nonprofit. This "sibling" structure enables pursuing both commercial applications and public goods without a direct hierarchical relationship.

The for-profit focuses on AI-native engineering software and defense contracts, while the nonprofit supports ecosystem development through research and education. This separation allows each entity to optimize for its mission while maintaining strategic alignment. However, the lack of formal control relationships creates coordination challenges and potential mission drift.

#### Arc Institute: Endowment-Powered Research

Arc Institute, launched in 2021 with $650 million from technology entrepreneurs, demonstrates the power of substantial, unrestricted funding. Core Investigators receive eight-year, renewable appointments with full salary support and no grant requirements, enabling them to pursue ambitious projects without funding anxiety.

Arc's model combines academic freedom with the urgency of a startup. Investigators maintain university affiliations while working at Arc's dedicated facility. Technology centers provide shared infrastructure and expertise. Physical co-location and a flat hierarchy accelerate collaboration beyond traditional academic departments.

However, Arc's dependence on initial philanthropic capital raises questions about scalability. The model requires enormous upfront investment with uncertain sustainability mechanisms. Success depends on continued donor commitment rather than any form of internal revenue generation.

### 2.3 Alternative Models: Open Source and Beyond

#### OpenBCI: Commercial Open Source

OpenBCI demonstrates that open-source hardware companies can achieve commercial sustainability while maintaining mission commitment. Since 2013, the company has sold brain-computer interface devices while releasing all designs under open licenses.

The business model focuses on monetizing convenience and quality assurance, rather than relying on exclusive IP. Pre-assembled boards cost 2-3x component prices, with premiums covering testing, documentation, and support. This approach generated approximately $4 million in annual revenue, supporting 26 employees while enabling a global community of developers and researchers.

OpenBCI's success proves that radical transparency can create a competitive advantage through ecosystem effects. However, as a traditional for-profit corporation, nothing prevents future leadership from restricting access or prioritizing profits. Recent venture funding raises questions about whether open-source commitments can survive the pressures of growth.

The OpenBCI case illustrates both potential and limitations of mission-driven for-profits. While culture and leadership can maintain mission alignment, structural safeguards provide stronger protection against drift. This insight informs the design of frameworks that combine commercial sustainability with a permanent mission commitment.

## 3\. Comparative Analysis: Strengths, Weaknesses, and Lessons Learned

### 3.1 ARPA Models: Power and Limitations

**Strengths:**

- Empowered program managers drive visionary portfolio development  
- Risk tolerance enables breakthrough innovations impossible elsewhere  
- Active management ensures rapid iteration and course correction  
- Proven track record of transformative impact (internet, GPS, mRNA vaccines)  
- Flexible funding allows the pursuit of emerging opportunities

**Weaknesses:**

- Dependence on exceptional individuals creates scalability constraints  
- Knowledge loss when program managers rotate out after 3-5 years  
- Difficulty maintaining continuity across program transitions  
- Limited mechanisms for technology transition to deployment  
- No inherent commitment to equitable access or global distribution


**Key Lessons:** ARPA models excel at catalyzing breakthroughs but struggle with sustained development and deployment. The program manager model creates dynamism but also instability. Success depends heavily on individual vision rather than institutional structures.

### 3.2 FROs: Focused Excellence with Transition Challenges

**Strengths:**

- Concentrated effort on specific bottlenecks achieves rapid progress  
- Full-time teams enable sustained engineering beyond academic capabilities  
- Clear milestones and finite timelines maintain focus and urgency  
- Nonprofit structure ensures public benefit orientation  
- Open science defaults accelerate field-wide advancement

**Weaknesses:**

- Inability to offer equity compensation limits talent recruitment  
- Fixed end dates create instability as the conclusion approaches  
- No clear transition path from research to deployment  
- Difficulty expanding scope beyond initial problem definition  
- Sustainability challenges without recurring revenue mechanisms

**Key Lessons:** FROs demonstrate that targeted efforts can effectively resolve specific bottlenecks. However, the model's strengths (focus, time limits) also create weaknesses (inflexibility, instability). The absence of equity participation and deployment pathways limits potential impact.

### 3.3 Wellcome Leap: Global Ambition with Structural Gaps

**Strengths:**

- Massive scale ($650+ million) enables ambitious global programs  
- Sophisticated agreements balance open science with commercialization  
- Step-in rights ensure technologies reach deployment  
- Global reach through a distributed performer network  
- Milestone-based management maintains accountability with flexibility

**Weaknesses:**

- No predefined commercial entity delays equity participation  
- Talent recruitment challenges without ownership incentives  
- The program's conclusion creates discontinuity in development  
- Dependence on Wellcome Trust endowment for sustainability  
- Limited mechanisms for performer capacity building beyond programs


**Key Lessons:** Wellcome Leap proves that DARPA principles can address global health challenges at scale. The sophisticated IP frameworks and step-in rights provide essential precedents. However, the absence of predefined commercial structures and equity participation mechanisms limits talent attraction and long-term sustainability.

### 3.4 Hybrid Models: Innovation with Accountability Challenges

**Strengths:**

- Flexible structures enable diverse revenue streams  
- Can attract both philanthropic and investment capital  
- Maintain mission focus while building sustainability  
- Demonstrate that investors accept capped returns  
- Enable technology development at a commercial scale

**Weaknesses:**

- Complex governance creates stakeholder confusion  
- Mission enforcement depends on leadership rather than structure  
- Retroactive restructuring faces legal challenges  
- Accountability gaps in private structures (LLCs)  
- Potential conflicts between profit and purpose

**Key Lessons:** Hybrid models prove that organizations can attract substantial capital while maintaining mission focus. However, structural design from inception proves critical—retroactive changes face legal and practical obstacles. A clear hierarchical control and legal mission-locking provide stronger protection than governance alone.

### 3.5 Cross-Model Insights

**Common Strengths Across Models:**

- Concentrated resources enable breakthrough progress  
- Mission focus attracts exceptional talent despite compensation constraints  
- Interdisciplinary teams solve complex challenges  
- Active management outperforms passive grant-making  
- Open science accelerates field advancement


**Common Weaknesses Across Models:**

- Talent retention without equity participation remains challenging  
- Transition from research to deployment lacks clear pathways  
- Sustainability beyond initial funding remains uncertain  
- Institutional knowledge preservation proves difficult  
- Global equity and access often remain secondary considerations

**Critical Gaps Requiring Innovation:**

- **Equity Participation:** Need mechanisms providing ownership benefits without compromising nonprofit status  
- **Phase Transitions:** Require clear pathways from research through deployment  
- **Sustainable Revenue:** Must develop recurring income beyond philanthropy  
- **Mission Protection:** Need structural rather than governance-based safeguards  
- **Global Access:** Require built-in mechanisms ensuring equitable distribution

## 4\. The Helix Framework: Integration and Innovation

**Desiderata**

1. **Expanded Time-Span Beyond the Derisking Phase:** The organization will have a planning horizon that substantially exceeds the typical five-year timeframe and includes clear transition milestones, strategic objectives, and a well-defined plan for successful organizational exits, utilizing a hybrid structure (e.g., licensing of intellectual property to for-profit arms with specified commercialization strategies).  
2. **Long-term Mission-Aligned Deliverables:** The structure will reconcile a long-term vision and mission (characteristic of academic endeavors) with the need for short-term deliverables (common in corporate settings) that provide rapid, frequent performance metrics for planning. This ensures nimble, adaptive alignment with a continuously shifting landscape while maintaining the trajectory toward the overarching long-term vision.  
3. **Objective Metric-Based Performance Assessment:** The organization will employ quantitative and objective performance metrics, such as Objectives and Key Results (OKRs) and Key Performance Indicators (KPIs), to rigorously track progress against defined milestones.  
4. **Operational Autonomy and Agility:** The model mandates structural independence and streamlined processes for strategic and financial planning, enabling rapid execution, minimizing organizational bureaucracy, and facilitating seamless pivoting or redirection as needed (inspired by models like the NSF Tech Labs).  
5. **Collaborative Ecosystem Integration:** The organization will actively establish and maintain an extensive network of collaborations (e.g., Wellcome Leap HBNet) to enable efficient sharing of efforts, discoveries, and resources while proactively establishing standardized mechanisms for addressing complex issues, such as Intellectual Property (IP) sharing, funding distribution, and unique healthcare challenges like human data access and privacy.  
6. **Public Stakeholder Investment and Equitable Reciprocity:** The public ("people") is treated as an early investor to cultivate a sustainable ecosystem. This requires developing a framework to map organizational stages across traditional for-profit and impact-focused companies and determine a "fair share" return for the public. This share will be strategically reinvested in subsequent R\&D, grants, and policy initiatives, or used to subsidize the pricing of successfully commercialized products to ensure equitable access for the broader community.

### 4.1 Foundational Philosophy

The Helix Framework emerges from a fundamental recognition: profit and purpose are not opposing forces that require balance, but complementary strands of organizational DNA that strengthen each other through their intertwining. Like the double helix, where two strands stabilize the overall structure while maintaining distinct identities, mission-driven organizations achieve maximum impact when commercial success and social benefit spiral upward together.

This philosophy transcends traditional dichotomies between nonprofit and for-profit, between financial returns and social impact, and between sustainability and accessibility. The Framework recognizes that in healthcare, doing good and doing well are not parallel paths but integrated requirements for transformational impact.

### 4.2 Core Components of the Helix Framework

#### Component 1: Hierarchical Mission-Locked Structure

The Framework mandates a hierarchical structure in which the nonprofit parent maintains absolute control over all subsidiaries. Unlike sibling structures (CZI) or unclear relationships (OpenAI's early model), this hierarchy ensures mission primacy in all decisions.

All for-profit subsidiaries must be incorporated as Public Benefit Corporations from inception. This legal requirement to balance shareholder interests with public benefit cannot be removed without supermajority approval, creating a structural mission-locking mechanism that extends beyond governance alone.

The nonprofit parent maintains control through:

- Majority board representation in all subsidiaries  
- Veto rights over fundamental changes  
- Mission-protective provisions in all organizational documents  
- Regular impact assessment and reporting requirements

#### Component 2: Future Interest Participation Agreements (FIPAs)

Addressing the talent challenge faced by FROs and Wellcome Leap, the Framework introduces FIPAs—contractual rights to participate in future value creation. These instruments provide equity-like upside without compromising nonprofit status during research phases.

FIPAs operate through staged conversion:

- During the research phase: Contractual participation rights vest over time  
- At commercialization: Rights convert to equity in mission-locked PBC subsidiaries  
- Value realization: Participants benefit from commercial success while maintaining mission alignment

This innovation enables organizations to compete for top talent while maintaining a charitable purpose throughout development phases.

#### Component 3: Quantitative Phase Transitions

The Framework establishes predetermined, evidence-based criteria for organizational evolution, preventing both premature commercialization and analysis paralysis.

**Research Phase Requirements:**

- Peer-reviewed validation of core science  
- Proof of concept in controlled settings  
- Initial safety assessments  
- Clear pathway to application  
- Quantified impact potential

**Development Phase Triggers:**

- Technical systems at 99% reliability  
- Regulatory pathway confirmed  
- Manufacturing feasibility demonstrated  
- Clinical protocols approved  
- Risk-benefit profile validated

**Deployment Phase Criteria:**

- Efficacy proven in trials  
- Safety profile confirmed  
- Cost-effectiveness demonstrated  
- Access infrastructure established  
- Five-year sustainability plan validated

These quantitative gates ensure technologies advance based on evidence rather than funding pressures or perfectionism.

#### Component 4: Graduated Return Architecture

Building on OpenAI's capped-profit innovation while addressing its weaknesses, the Framework implements sophisticated return structures:

**Return Cap Tiers:**

- Seed investors (highest risk): Up to 100x returns  
- Early investors (high risk): Up to 50x returns  
- Growth investors (moderate risk): Up to 10x returns  
- Late investors (low risk): Up to 2x returns  
- All excess returns flow to charitable purposes

**Distribution Waterfall:**

- Operating expenses and reasonable reserves  
- Capped returns per investor agreements  
- R\&D reinvestment  
- Global access programs  
- Excess to the parent nonprofit


This structure attracts necessary capital while ensuring the majority of value creation serves public benefit.

#### Component 5: Global Access at the Core

The Framework embeds health equity through structural rather than voluntary mechanisms:

**Tiered Pricing Architecture:**

- Low-income countries (World Bank classification): Free or at-cost  
- Lower-middle-income: Cost plus minimal margin  
- Upper-middle-income: Purchasing power parity pricing  
- High-income: Commercial rates subsidizing global access

**Technology Reserve Requirements:**

- Core platforms are maintained for emergency deployment  
- Manufacturing protocols pre-shared with qualified producers  
- Regulatory packages prepared for rapid submission  
- Automatic licensing during health emergencies

**Capacity Building Obligations:**

- Technology transfer to developing country partners  
- Local manufacturing requirements  
- Training and support programs  
- Revenue sharing with origin communities

#### Component 6: Triple-Layer Accountability

The Framework implements redundant accountability mechanisms, preventing mission drift:

**Structural Layer:**

- Legal requirements in the founding documents  
- PBC structure for all commercial entities  
- Immutable mission provisions  
- Return caps and distribution requirements

**Community Layer:**

- Patient advisory boards with governance powers  
- Beneficiary representation requirements  
- Public comment periods  
- Transparent development processes

**External Layer:**

- Independent annual reviews  
- Third-party impact verification  
- Regulatory compliance monitoring  
- Whistleblower protections

Any two layers can block actions compromising the mission, ensuring robust protection against drift.

### 4.3 Operational Innovations

#### Continuous Evolution Model

Unlike time-bound FROs or program-limited ARPA models, the Helix Framework enables continuous evolution through predetermined phases. Organizations maintain institutional knowledge while adapting to changing contexts, preventing the discontinuity that undermines sustained impact.

#### Parallel Development Tracks

Rather than sequential progression, the Framework enables parallel advancement:

- Core research continues under nonprofit protection  
- Commercial applications develop in PBC subsidiaries  
- Regulatory processes begin early  
- Market development proceeds simultaneously

This parallelization accelerates time to impact while maintaining mission protection.

#### Emergency Activation Protocols

Learning from COVID-19, the Framework includes predetermined emergency provisions:

- WHO emergency declarations trigger automatic licensing  
- Cost-based pricing activates in affected regions  
- Manufacturing partners receive immediate authorization  
- Regulatory fast-tracks engage automatically

These protocols eliminate negotiation delays during crises.

#### Revenue Diversification Strategy

The Framework mandates multiple revenue streams:

- Philanthropic grants for basic research  
- Government contracts for development  
- Commercial revenues from developed markets  
- Impact investments with capped returns  
- Service revenues from platform access

This diversification ensures resilience and prevents capture by single stakeholders.

### 4.4 Integration of Best Practices

The Helix Framework synthesizes strengths from analyzed models while addressing their weaknesses:

**From ARPA Models:**

- Empowered leadership driving vision  
- Risk tolerance for breakthrough innovation  
- Active management and rapid iteration  
- BUT adds institutional continuity and equity participation

**From FROs:**

- Focused effort on specific challenges  
- Full-time dedicated teams  
- Clear milestones and accountability  
- BUT adds transition pathways and ownership incentives

**From Wellcome Leap:**

- Global scale and ambition  
- Sophisticated IP frameworks  
- Network-based capabilities  
- BUT adds predefined commercial structures and equity participation

**From Hybrid Models:**

- Multiple entity types for flexibility  
- Investor participation with mission protection  
- Revenue generation for sustainability  
- BUT adds structural safeguards and a clear hierarchy

**From Open Source Models:**

- Transparency and community engagement  
- Alternative monetization strategies  
- Ecosystem value creation  
- BUT adds mission-locking and equity participation

## 5\. The Cytognosis Foundation: First Implementation of the Helix Framework

### 5.1 Organizational Architecture

Cytognosis Foundation, incorporated in Delaware on September 8, 2025, as a 501(c)(3) nonprofit public benefit corporation, represents the first comprehensive implementation of the Helix Framework. The organization demonstrates the practical application of theoretical principles while pioneering additional innovations tailored to healthcare-specific requirements.

#### Hierarchical Structure Implementation

The Foundation maintains absolute control over all subsidiary entities through specific governance mechanisms:

- Nonprofit parent holds 100% voting control of initial subsidiaries  
- All subsidiaries must be Delaware Public Benefit Corporations  
- Board representation ensures a minimum of 60% nonprofit appointees  
- Veto rights over fundamental changes, including mission modifications  
- Quarterly impact assessments against predetermined metrics

The founding Certificate of Incorporation and Bylaws embed these requirements irreversibly, preventing future boards from compromising the mission regardless of stakeholder pressure.

#### Board Composition and Expertise

The initial board reflects the Framework's diversity requirements:

- **Dr. Shahin Mohammadi** (Chair, CEO): Patient-founder with 15+ years pioneering AI for biology at both industry (insitro, GenBio AI) and academia (MIT, Broad Institute, Purdue University)  
- **Dr. Ananth Grama** (Vice Chair): Distinguished Professor and Director of Purdue Institute for Physical AI, with pioneering work in scalable computation.  
- **Dr. Patricia Purcell** (Secretary, Director of Development): Harvard-trained geneticist with extensive experience managing large lab teams (\>40 personnel) and grant budgets (totaling\>$22 million)  
- **Dr. Madhvi Menon** (Board Scientific Advisor, Immunoverse Lead): Group Leader at the University of Manchester, investigating B cell-microenvironment interactions in chronic disease.

The planned expansion includes patient advocates (minimum 25% of the board), global health practitioners from target regions, regulatory experts familiar with FDA and international requirements, bioethicists to ensure responsible innovation, and community representatives from underserved populations.

### 5.2 Financial Innovation in Practice

#### Future Interest Participation Agreements

Cytognosis has developed the first legal framework for FIPAs, working with Delaware counsel to ensure compliance with nonprofit regulations while providing meaningful participation rights. Early employees receive:

- Contractual rights vesting over four years  
- Participation scaled to risk and contribution level  
- Conversion to PBC equity upon commercialization  
- Anti-dilution protections through Series A  
- Accelerated vesting upon acquisition or IPO

This structure has already proven attractive to senior engineers who rejected traditional startup offers, validating the Framework's hypothesis that mission-aligned ownership can compete with pure equity compensation.

#### Graduated Return Implementation

Cytognosis's funding strategy demonstrates the graduated return architecture:

**Seed Phase (Current):**

- Seeking $3-5 million at $10-15 million valuation  
- 100x return cap for earliest investors  
- Focus on philanthropic investors and mission-aligned angels

**Series A Planning (2026):**

- Target $30-40 million at $60-80 million pre-money  
- 50x return cap reflecting reduced risk  
- Blend of impact investors and strategic partners

**Growth Phases (2027+):**

- Decreasing caps as technology validates  
- Increasing institutional participation  
- Clear path to sustainability by Year 5

Early investor conversations confirm acceptance of capped returns in exchange for participation in transformational health technology with guaranteed mission alignment.

### 5.3 Technology Development Strategy

#### The Neuroverse and Immunoverse Platforms

Cytognosis develops two foundational platforms demonstrating parallel track development:

**Neuroverse:** AI-native monitoring of neurological health through continuous biomarker analysis. The platform maps neurological states to enable early detection of conditions from Alzheimer's to stroke.

**Immunoverse:** Comprehensive immune system profiling predicting disease susceptibility and treatment response. The system decodes immune memory to understand individual health trajectories.

Both platforms advance simultaneously under nonprofit protection while specific applications develop in PBC subsidiaries. This parallel approach accelerates development while maintaining mission focus.

#### Quantitative Transition Criteria

Cytognosis has established specific phase gates:

**Research Phase (Current \- 2026):**

- Publish 3+ peer-reviewed papers validating core algorithms  
- Achieve 90% sensitivity/specificity in controlled studies  
- Complete IRB-approved pilot studies with 100+ participants  
- File provisional patents on key innovations  
- Demonstrate 10x improvement over current methods

**Development Phase (2026-2028):**

- Scale to 1,000+ participant studies  
- Achieve FDA Breakthrough Device designation  
- Establish manufacturing partnerships on three continents  
- Validate health economic benefits  
- Secure strategic partnerships with health systems

**Deployment Phase (2028+):**

- Complete pivotal clinical trials  
- Obtain regulatory approvals in the US, EU, and priority markets  
- Launch commercial operations in high-income markets  
- Initiate subsidized programs in developing countries  
- Achieve operational sustainability

### 5.4 Global Health Implementation

#### Tiered Access from Inception

Cytognosis embeds global access in all planning:

**Technology Design:**

- Algorithms optimized for low-resource settings  
- Minimal infrastructure requirements  
- Offline capabilities for intermittent connectivity  
- Cultural adaptation frameworks

**Partnership Development:**

- MOUs with health ministries in Kenya, India, and Brazil  
- Collaboration with the WHO on emergency preparedness  
- Training programs for local healthcare workers  
- Revenue sharing with data origin communities

**Pricing Architecture:**

- Free access in 47 UN-designated least developed countries  
- At-cost pricing in lower-middle-income countries  
- PPP-adjusted pricing in upper-middle-income countries  
- Commercial rates in high-income markets subsidize global programs


#### Emergency Preparedness Integration

Cytognosis's bylaws include automatic emergency provisions:

- Predetermined triggers based on WHO declarations  
- Immediate licensing to qualified manufacturers  
- Cost-based pricing in affected regions  
- Expedited regulatory cooperation protocols  
- Pre-positioned technology reserves

These provisions are contractually binding on all investors and cannot be overridden during times of crisis.

### 5.5 Accountability and Transparency

#### Triple-Layer Implementation

**Structural Accountability:**

- Delaware PBC requirements for all subsidiaries  
- Immutable mission provisions in the founding documents  
- Return caps specified in all investment agreements  
- Mandatory impact reporting requirements

**Community Accountability:**

- Community Advisory Board with formal powers (forming Q2 2026\)  
- Patient representatives on the main board (recruitment ongoing)  
- Public comment periods for major decisions  
- Open development sprints with stakeholder input

**External Accountability:**

- Annual B-Corp certification process (planned 2027\)  
- Third-party impact audits (beginning 2026\)  
- Voluntary disclosure exceeding legal requirements  
- Independent ombudsperson function (when scaled)

#### Transparent Operations

Cytognosis commits to radical transparency:

- Publishing annual impact reports with verified metrics  
- Disclosing executive compensation  
- Sharing aggregated health insights with the research community  
- Open-sourcing non-competitive algorithms and tools  
- Regular community updates on progress and challenges

### 5.6 Early Validation and Future Outlook

#### Stakeholder Response

Early implementation has validated key Framework hypotheses:

- Investors accept capped returns for mission-aligned opportunities  
- Engineers value FIPAs as meaningful ownership alternatives  
- Partners appreciate structural mission guarantees  
- Communities welcome genuine governance participation

#### Challenges and Adaptations

Implementation has revealed areas requiring refinement:

- FIPA legal structures need state-by-state validation  
- Community governance mechanisms require capacity building  
- International operations need localized legal frameworks  
- Technology transfer agreements need standardization

#### Scaling the Framework

Cytognosis serves as proof-of-concept for broader Framework adoption. Planned initiatives include:

- Publishing open-source legal templates  
- Creating implementation guides for other organizations  
- Building practitioner community for shared learning  
- Advocating for regulatory frameworks supporting mission-locked innovation

## Conclusion

The Helix Framework represents a fundamental reimagining of how organizations can pursue breakthrough innovation while maintaining an unwavering commitment to public benefit. By recognizing that profit and purpose are complementary strands of an organization's DNA, rather than opposing forces, the Framework enables structures where commercial success amplifies social impact in an upward spiral of sustainable transformation.

Through a comprehensive analysis of existing models—from ARPA's empowered program managers to FROs' targeted excellence and Wellcome Leap's global ambitions—we identified critical gaps that prevent sustained impact at scale. The Helix Framework addresses these gaps through six integrated components: hierarchical mission-locked structures, Future Interest Participation Agreements, quantitative phase transitions, graduated return architectures, global access DNA, and triple-layer accountability.

The Cytognosis Foundation's implementation demonstrates practical feasibility while revealing areas for continued refinement. Early validation confirms that stakeholders across the spectrum—investors, employees, partners, and communities—embrace structures that ensure mission alignment while enabling sustainable growth and scaling.

As Dr. Shahin Mohammadi, founder of Cytognosis Foundation, articulates: 

"*Capital is oxygen for moonshots—without it, transformative ideas suffocate before reaching the patients they could save. But here's the paradox: the moment money shifts from catalyst to purpose, we fail. True innovation emerges when mission alignment is encoded in our organizational DNA, as immutable as genetic code itself. Like the double helix, profit and purpose spiral upward together—neither can exist without the other, each stabilizing and propelling its complement. One funds the journey, the other defines the destination, but both are expressions of the same underlying life force. That's the alchemy: where capital becomes the engine of equity, where opposing forces reveal themselves as dance partners, and where the impossible becomes inevitable.*"

The path forward requires continued experimentation, careful documentation of successes and failures, and a willingness to adapt structures as contexts evolve. The Helix Framework provides not a rigid template but a living architecture that evolves while maintaining its essential character—much like the DNA it's named after.

Future research should examine Framework adaptation across different domains, optimal parameter calibration for various risk profiles, mechanisms for international coordination, and regulatory innovations supporting mission-locked structures. As more organizations implement these principles, collective learning will strengthen and refine the model, ultimately enhancing its effectiveness.

The ultimate measure of success will be lives saved, suffering prevented, and inequities eliminated. In a world where zip code predicts life expectancy more accurately than genetic code, where treatable diseases kill millions for lack of access, and where breakthrough innovations remain locked behind economic barriers, new organizational models are not merely innovative but morally imperative. The Helix Framework offers hope that we can harness the power of markets and technology for genuine public benefit, creating a future in which advanced health technologies serve all humanity rather than merely those who can pay.

## Acknowledgments

The authors thank the teams at Convergent Research, OpenAI, Wellcome Leap, and Chan Zuckerberg Initiative for their pioneering work and for transparently sharing lessons learned. Special recognition goes to early advisors and supporters of the Cytognosis Foundation who validated and refined the Helix Framework through practical implementation.

# **Appendix**

## Innovation Landscape Map

In the Harvard Business Review article “[You Need an Innovation Strategy](https://www2.qa.hbr.org/2015/06/you-need-an-innovation-strategy),” Gary P. Pisano introduced the “Innovation Landscape Map,” which considers innovations along two dimensions: the degree to which they involve a change in business model and the degree to which they involve a change in technology.

# **Parking Lot**

* Who to invite? Where to publish?

[2\. Stokes, D. E. *Pasteur’s Quadrant: Basic Science and Technological Innovation*. (Brookings Institution Press, Washington, D.C., 1997).](https://app.readcube.com/library/?style=Nature+{"language":"en-US"})
